ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for duck noodle soup; mapped to closest parent noodle dish class FOODON_00002473. No exact or more specific FoodOn term for duck noodle soup was identified as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers noodle soup dishes broadly; no duck-specific noodle soup code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024. Mapped to closest parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for duck noodle soup as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of duck noodle soup in Singapore is approximately 400 g, comprising noodles, braised duck meat and skin, green vegetables, and broth.

nutrient_source.title: Duck noodles (soup) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo entry for Duck noodles (soup), CR ID F05060046. All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Egg Noodles and Mixed Noodle Dishes — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for duck noodle soup as a composite dish. GI of 58 (medium) is estimated using egg noodles as the primary carbohydrate vehicle, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database entries for egg noodle-based dishes (GI range 46–61). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 33.04) / 100 ≈ 19.2, based on 400 g serving providing 33.04 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 358 mg/100 g with negligible potassium and fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size, avoid drinking the soup broth, and request reduced-sodium broth preparation, as sodium at 358 mg/100 g accumulates to approximately 1,432 mg per 400 g bowl — exceeding 60% of the 2,300 mg daily limit in a single meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined egg noodles with low dietary fibre (0.57 g/100 g) and medium GI of 58
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate portion size of noodles, consider requesting wholemeal or lower-GI noodle substitutes, and increase the proportion of duck meat and green vegetables to reduce the glycemic load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (358 mg/100 g) and duck skin contributing saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request the dish without duck skin to reduce saturated fat intake, avoid consuming the broth to limit sodium load, and limit consumption frequency given the combined sodium and saturated fat burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 358 mg/100 g approaching RA threshold with no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request reduced-sodium broth, add leafy greens or bok choy for antioxidants and fibre, and note that the dish provides no material omega-3 or polyphenol content to offset inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Duck Noodle Soup is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker dish consisting of thin egg noodles served in a clear or lightly seasoned broth, topped with shredded braised duck meat, duck skin, and green vegetables such as bok choy or chye sim. The duck is typically slow-braised in a soy-based master stock seasoned with five-spice, garlic, and sometimes ginger, yielding tender, flavourful meat with a savoury-umami profile. The broth is rich in sodium from soy sauce and braising liquid. Per 100 g, the dish provides 67 kcal, 4.23 g protein, 1.92 g fat, and 8.26 g carbohydrate, with 358 mg sodium and only 0.57 g dietary fibre. The medium glycemic index of approximately 58 reflects the refined egg noodle base. Clinically, the dish is a concern for hypertension due to high sodium content concentrated in the broth, and for Type 2 Diabetes due to the low-fibre refined noodle base. It is not suitable for patients requiring low-sodium or low-GI diets without modification. The dish is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal in Singapore hawker centres and coffee shops.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in duck noodle soup?
Can diabetics eat duck noodle soup?
Is duck noodle soup high in calories?
What are the nutrients in duck noodle soup?
Is duck noodle soup suitable for hypertension?
How many calories in a bowl of duck noodle soup?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F05060046) at confidence 0.95. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous egg noodle entries. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and supported.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this composite duck noodle soup dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F05060046 as of 2026. Saturated fat estimated at 0.55 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central duck meat and egg noodle composition data as a plausible secondary estimate. Input cultural tags (primary_cuisine: western, dietary_tags: vegetarian, plant_based) were incorrect for this dish and have been corrected to Singaporean Chinese cuisine; the dish contains duck meat and is not vegetarian or plant-based. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on egg noodle-based dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for duck noodle soup was found in approved sources. Ontology match types corrected from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the permitted enumeration values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] required by the scoring model.
